name: AP Chemistry
description: Master the full AP Chemistry curriculum — from atomic structure to electrochemistry. Covers all 9 CED units aligned to the College Board AP Chemistry Course and Exam Description effective Fall 2024.

facts.quizQuestion: How many particles are in exactly one mole of any substance?
facts.explanation: Avogadro's number (6.02214076 × 10^23 mol⁻¹) defines the mole in the 2019 SI redefinition. It equals the number of atoms in exactly 12 grams of carbon-12. The number is named after Amedeo Avogadro, though it was experimentally determined after his death.
facts.statement: One mole of any substance contains 6.022 × 10^23 particles (Avogadro's number).
facts.wowFactor: If you had a mole of seconds, it would be longer than the age of the universe by a factor of about 100 million.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing spherical atom cluster with the number 6.022 × 10^23 etched in gold on a dark laboratory chalkboard surrounded by periodic table elements

facts.quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the mole (mol) measure in the SI system?
facts.explanation: The mole is the SI base unit for amount of substance. One mole is defined as exactly 6.02214076 × 10^23 elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions, etc.). It bridges the microscopic world of atoms to the macroscopic world of grams we can measure in the lab.
facts.statement: The mole is the SI base unit for amount of substance.
facts.wowFactor: The mole was redefined in 2019 to have an exact numerical value — for the first time, a unit defined by a fixed number rather than a physical artifact.
facts.visualDescription: A balance scale with tiny glowing atoms on one side and a large gram weight on the other, pixel art style with a dark forge background

facts.quizQuestion: What is the molar mass of carbon (C) in g/mol?
facts.explanation: Carbon's standard atomic weight is 12.011 g/mol, reflecting the natural mix of carbon-12 (98.89%) and carbon-13 (1.11%). It is NOT exactly 12.000 — that would be pure carbon-12. The IUPAC value of 12.011 accounts for the natural isotope distribution.
facts.statement: The molar mass of carbon (C) is 12.011 g/mol.
facts.wowFactor: Carbon's molar mass is slightly above 12 because about 1 in 90 carbon atoms in nature is the heavier carbon-13 isotope.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing carbon atom with six protons shown in pixel art, molar mass label visible on a forge anvil

facts.quizQuestion: What is the molar mass of water (H₂O) in g/mol?
facts.explanation: Water (H₂O) has a molar mass of 18.02 g/mol: two hydrogen atoms (2 × 1.008 = 2.016) plus one oxygen atom (15.999) equals 18.015, rounded to 18.02. This means 18.02 grams of water contains exactly one mole (6.022 × 10^23 molecules) of H₂O.
facts.statement: The molar mass of water (H₂O) is 18.02 g/mol (2×1.008 + 15.999).
facts.wowFactor: A single teaspoon of water (about 5 mL, ~5 g) contains roughly 1.67 × 10^23 water molecules — nearly a third of a mole.
facts.visualDescription: Two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom bonding in pixel art, with a vial of water glowing blue and a molar mass label

facts.quizQuestion: To convert moles of a substance to grams, what operation do you perform?
facts.explanation: The conversion factor between moles and grams is molar mass (g/mol). Grams = moles × molar mass. For example, 2.00 mol of NaCl × 58.44 g/mol = 116.88 g. The molar mass acts as the bridge between the counting unit (mole) and the measurable mass (grams).
facts.statement: To convert moles to grams, multiply the number of moles by the molar mass (g/mol).
facts.wowFactor: The molar mass is the same number as the atomic or formula mass — just in different units (g/mol instead of u or amu).
facts.visualDescription: A dimensional analysis arrow diagram converting 'mol' to 'g' with a molar mass conversion factor shown in glowing text on a forge background

facts.quizQuestion: What is the molar mass of sodium chloride (NaCl) in g/mol?
facts.explanation: NaCl's molar mass = Na (22.990) + Cl (35.45) = 58.44 g/mol. This is why dissolving one mole of table salt requires weighing out 58.44 grams. The calculation always sums the atomic masses of all atoms in the formula unit.
facts.statement: Sodium chloride (NaCl) has a molar mass of 58.44 g/mol (Na 22.990 + Cl 35.45).
facts.wowFactor: A standard salt shaker holds about 100 g of NaCl — that is about 1.7 moles, containing more than 10^24 sodium and chloride ions.
facts.visualDescription: Sodium and chloride ions alternating in a crystal lattice pixel art, with formula NaCl and molar mass displayed on a stone tablet

facts.quizQuestion: How many molecules are in 2.00 moles of a substance?
facts.explanation: 2.00 mol × 6.022 × 10^23 molecules/mol = 1.204 × 10^24 molecules. This is a direct application of Avogadro's number as a conversion factor. The number of moles multiplied by Avogadro's number always gives the number of individual particles.
facts.statement: Two moles of any substance contains 2 × 6.022 × 10^23 = 1.204 × 10^24 particles.
facts.wowFactor: Multiplying by Avogadro's number is the only way chemists 'count' something too small to ever directly count.
facts.visualDescription: Two large glowing mole symbols with billions of tiny atom dots radiating outward in pixel art

facts.quizQuestion: What are the units of molar mass?
facts.explanation: Molar mass has units of grams per mole (g/mol). Numerically it equals the atomic or formula mass in atomic mass units (amu or u), but the units differ: amu is mass per atom/molecule, while g/mol is mass per mole of those particles. The numerical equality is a consequence of Avogadro's number definition.
facts.statement: Molar mass is expressed in grams per mole (g/mol).
facts.wowFactor: The numerical value of molar mass in g/mol exactly equals the atomic mass in amu — this elegant fact is not a coincidence but a deliberate consequence of how the mole was defined.
facts.visualDescription: A unit label 'g/mol' glowing in forge-fire orange over a balance scale in pixel art

acceptableAlternatives: same atomic number, different mass number
facts.quizQuestion: What defines isotopes of the equal element?
facts.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same atomic number = same number of protons) but with different numbers of neutrons, giving different mass numbers. For example, carbon-12 has 6 protons + 6 neutrons, while carbon-13 has 6 protons + 7 neutrons. Both are carbon; they just have different masses.
facts.statement: Isotopes of the same element have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
facts.wowFactor: Tin has 10 stable isotopes — the most of any element — meaning the 'average' tin atom described by its atomic mass doesn't exactly match any real tin atom.
facts.visualDescription: Two atom diagrams side by side — both labeled carbon — one with 6 neutrons, one with 7, neutron count highlighted in pixel art

facts.quizQuestion: What quantity is plotted on the x-axis of a mass spectrum?
facts.explanation: In mass spectrometry, the x-axis shows the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z). For singly ionized atoms (charge = +1), m/z equals the mass number. The y-axis shows relative abundance (the signal intensity of each ion). A mass spectrum of an element reveals its isotope distribution.
facts.statement: A mass spectrum plots mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) on the x-axis and relative abundance on the y-axis.
facts.wowFactor: Mass spectrometry is sensitive enough to distinguish isotopes atom by atom — the same technique is used both for AP Chemistry and for carbon-dating archaeological artifacts.
facts.visualDescription: A mass spectrum chart showing tall and short peaks at different m/z values, labeled axes glowing in pixel art laboratory setting

distractors: simple average of all isotope masses
mass number of most stable isotope
facts.quizQuestion: How is the the weighted mean atomic mass of an element calculated from mass spectrum data?
facts.explanation: Average atomic mass = Σ(isotope mass × fractional abundance). For chlorine: (34.969 amu × 0.7576) + (36.966 amu × 0.2424) = 35.45 amu. The values shown on the periodic table are these abundance-weighted averages, which is why they are rarely whole numbers.
facts.statement: Average atomic mass = sum of each (isotope mass × fractional abundance) across all isotopes.
facts.wowFactor: The periodic table value for chlorine (35.45) is not the mass of any real chlorine atom — it is a weighted average of Cl-35 and Cl-37 that no single atom actually has.
facts.visualDescription: A bar chart of chlorine isotope peaks, with a weighted average arrow pointing to 35.45 on a number line in pixel art

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately what percentage of natural carbon is the carbon-12 isotope?
facts.explanation: Carbon-12 makes up approximately 98.89% of natural carbon, with carbon-13 comprising about 1.11%. This is why carbon's average atomic mass (12.011 g/mol) is only slightly above 12.000 — the heavier C-13 slightly pulls the average upward.
facts.statement: Carbon-12 constitutes approximately 98.89% of naturally occurring carbon.
facts.wowFactor: The trace carbon-14 isotope (~1 in 10^12 carbon atoms) is radioactive and is the basis for radiocarbon dating spanning up to ~50,000 years.
facts.visualDescription: A large C-12 atom glowing brightly next to a small C-13 atom in pixel art, percentages labeled on a pie chart background

facts.quizQuestion: What are the two stable isotopes of chlorine?
facts.explanation: Chlorine has two stable isotopes: chlorine-35 (75.77% abundant, mass 34.969 amu) and chlorine-37 (24.23% abundant, mass 36.966 amu). Their weighted average gives chlorine its periodic table atomic mass of 35.45. A mass spectrum of chlorine shows two peaks in roughly a 3:1 ratio.
facts.statement: Chlorine's two stable isotopes are chlorine-35 (75.77%) and chlorine-37 (24.23%).
facts.wowFactor: Chlorine's two isotopes give its compounds a distinctive double-peak 'chlorine signature' in mass spectra — a fingerprint chemists use to identify chlorine-containing molecules.
facts.visualDescription: A mass spectrum showing two peaks at m/z 35 and 37 in a 3:1 ratio, labeled Cl-35 and Cl-37 in glowing pixel art

facts.quizQuestion: What quantity does the height of a peak in a mass spectrum represent?
facts.explanation: The y-axis of a mass spectrum shows relative abundance — the proportion of ions detected at each m/z value, normalized so the tallest peak (the base peak) equals 100%. The tallest peak corresponds to the most abundant isotope. Reading peak heights directly gives fractional abundances for the average atomic mass calculation.
facts.statement: Peak height in a mass spectrum represents the relative abundance of each isotope.
facts.wowFactor: Mass spectrometry detects single ions — the spectrum of a single element is essentially the instrument 'counting' individual atoms by mass.
facts.visualDescription: A mass spectrum with one tall peak and one shorter peak, a label 'relative abundance' on the y-axis in a pixel art laboratory

acceptableAlternatives: mass element over molar mass times 100
facts.quizQuestion: What formula gives the percent composition of an an atom type in a compound?
facts.explanation: Percent composition by mass = (total mass of element in one formula unit ÷ molar mass of compound) × 100%. For oxygen in H₂O: (15.999 ÷ 18.015) × 100% = 88.81%. This tells you what fraction of a compound's mass comes from each element.
facts.statement: Percent composition = (mass of element in formula unit / molar mass of compound) × 100%.
facts.wowFactor: By mass, water is 88.8% oxygen — so the element we use to put out fires makes up almost 9/10 of the mass of the substance we drink.
facts.visualDescription: A pie chart of water composition by mass — large oxygen wedge and small hydrogen wedge — in pixel art on a stone tablet

facts.quizQuestion: What does an empirical formula represent?
facts.explanation: The empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound. For example, glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) has an empirical formula of CH₂O. Empirical formulas are determined from percent composition data by converting mass percentages to moles and reducing to the smallest whole-number ratio.
facts.statement: An empirical formula shows the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound.
facts.wowFactor: Glucose and formaldehyde share the same empirical formula (CH₂O) but are completely different compounds — empirical formulas don't distinguish between them.
facts.visualDescription: Two molecules — glucose and formaldehyde — with the same CH₂O empirical formula label, highlighted in pixel art with a forge background

distractors: formula with all lone pairs shown
facts.quizQuestion: How does a molecular formula relate to the this value formula of the same compound?
facts.explanation: The molecular formula is always a whole-number multiple (n) of the empirical formula: molecular formula = n × (empirical formula). For H₂O₂ (hydrogen peroxide), the empirical formula is HO and n = 2. To find n, divide the compound's molar mass by the empirical formula mass.
facts.statement: Molecular formula = n × empirical formula, where n is determined by dividing molar mass by empirical formula mass.
facts.wowFactor: Acetylene (C₂H₂) and benzene (C₆H₆) have the same empirical formula (CH) but wildly different structures — one is a simple gas, the other an aromatic ring.
facts.visualDescription: Two molecular diagrams — HO empirical and H2O2 molecular — connected by a ×2 multiplier arrow in pixel art

facts.quizQuestion: In combustion analysis of a hydrocarbon, what two products are collected and measured?
facts.explanation: When a hydrocarbon (or carbon-hydrogen-oxygen compound) undergoes complete combustion, all carbon becomes CO₂ and all hydrogen becomes H₂O. The masses of CO₂ and H₂O collected are used to calculate the mass of C and H in the original sample, then the percent composition and empirical formula are determined.
facts.statement: Complete combustion of hydrocarbons produces CO₂ (from carbon) and H₂O (from hydrogen).
facts.wowFactor: Combustion analysis was the workhorse of organic chemistry for over 150 years — chemists burned thousands of compounds to determine molecular formulas before modern NMR and mass spectrometry existed.
facts.visualDescription: A combustion apparatus with a hydrocarbon burning and two collection traps labeled CO₂ and H₂O in pixel art

facts.quizQuestion: What is the percent by mass of carbon in carbon dioxide (CO₂)?
facts.explanation: Molar mass of CO₂ = 12.011 + 2(15.999) = 44.009 g/mol. Percent C = (12.011 / 44.009) × 100% = 27.29%. The remainder (72.71%) is oxygen. This is a classic percent composition calculation — the answer is often surprising because CO₂ is mostly oxygen by mass, not carbon.
facts.statement: Carbon makes up 27.29% of CO₂ by mass; oxygen makes up 72.71%.
facts.wowFactor: Despite being named 'carbon dioxide,' CO₂ is nearly 73% oxygen by mass — the carbon atom is far lighter than the two oxygen atoms surrounding it.
facts.visualDescription: A CO₂ molecule with carbon atom labeled 27% and two oxygen atoms labeled 73% in glowing pixel art percentage labels

facts.correctAnswer: convert to moles, find ratio, simplify
acceptableAlternatives: mass to moles then reduce ratio
percent to moles then simplest ratio
distractors: divide by atomic number, then reduce
convert percent to grams, then multiply
sum all masses, then divide by element count
multiply percent by molar mass, then round
use Avogadro's number to count atoms
find mass spectrum peaks, then compare
subtract oxygen mass from total, then divide
facts.quizQuestion: What is the correct sequence of steps to determine an empirical formula from percent composition data?
facts.explanation: Step 1: Assume 100 g sample, so percent values become gram values directly. Step 2: Convert each element's mass to moles (divide by molar mass). Step 3: Divide all mole values by the smallest mole value. Step 4: Round to whole numbers (or multiply all by the same integer if needed) to get the empirical formula subscripts.
facts.statement: To find an empirical formula: assume 100 g, convert mass to moles, divide by the smallest mole value, and round to whole numbers.
facts.wowFactor: The trick of assuming 100 g is one of chemistry's most elegant shortcuts — it instantly turns percent signs into gram values with no extra calculation.
facts.visualDescription: A step-by-step flowchart: percentages → grams → moles → ratio → empirical formula, shown in pixel art on a stone tablet

acceptableAlternatives: component mass over total mass times 100
facts.quizQuestion: How is mass percent (mass fraction × 100) of a this value in a mixture defined?
facts.explanation: Mass percent = (mass of component / total mass of mixture) × 100%. For example, if a mixture contains 5.0 g NaCl in 95.0 g water (100.0 g total), the mass percent of NaCl is (5.0/100.0) × 100% = 5.0%. Mass percent is the most common way to express concentration for solid-in-solid or solid-in-liquid mixtures.
facts.statement: Mass percent of a component = (mass of component / total mass of mixture) × 100%.
facts.wowFactor: Saline IV bags used in hospitals are typically 0.9% NaCl by mass — carefully controlled because even small deviations disrupt cell osmosis.
facts.visualDescription: A beaker with a solute particle cluster labeled with its mass fraction as a percentage, in pixel art with forge lighting

distractors: mass of component / moles of mixture
moles of solvent / moles of solute
facts.quizQuestion: How is the mole fraction (χ) of a component in a mixture defined?
facts.explanation: Mole fraction (χ_i) = moles of component i / total moles of all components in the mixture. Mole fractions are dimensionless and always sum to 1 for all components in a mixture. For a 1:3 mole ratio mixture of A and B, χ_A = 0.25 and χ_B = 0.75.
facts.statement: Mole fraction χ = moles of component / total moles in mixture. All mole fractions in a mixture sum to 1.
facts.wowFactor: Mole fraction is used instead of mass percent in gas laws and colligative properties because gases behave according to particle count, not mass.
facts.visualDescription: A mixture container with colored particles, each particle type labeled with its mole fraction in pixel art

acceptableAlternatives: pure mass over total mass times 100
facts.quizQuestion: How is the percent purity of a sample calculated?
facts.explanation: Percent purity = (mass of pure substance / total mass of sample) × 100%. A 95% pure sample of NaCl means that in every 100 g of material, 95 g is NaCl and 5 g is impurities. Purity calculations are critical in pharmaceutical manufacturing and when doing stoichiometry with impure reagents.
facts.statement: Percent purity = (mass of pure substance / total sample mass) × 100%.
facts.wowFactor: Pharmaceutical-grade reagents must be ≥ 99.9% pure — even 0.1% of the wrong substance could change a drug's dose or safety profile.
facts.visualDescription: A sample vial labeled '95% pure NaCl' with a small impurity cluster highlighted in red in pixel art

facts.quizQuestion: What must the sum of all mole fractions in any mixture equal?
facts.explanation: The sum of all mole fractions in a mixture always equals exactly 1 (or 100% if expressed as percentages). This is because each mole fraction is that component's share of the total moles — summing all shares accounts for all moles. This serves as a useful check: if your mole fractions don't sum to 1, there is a calculation error.
facts.statement: The sum of all mole fractions in any mixture is always equal to 1.
facts.wowFactor: This is a conservation law in disguise — mole fractions partition 100% of the matter in a mixture, so they must always add to 1, no exceptions.
facts.visualDescription: A pie chart divided into labeled mole fraction wedges that sum to one whole circle, pixel art style

facts.quizQuestion: What distinguishes a mixture from a pure substance in terms of this process?
facts.explanation: A mixture has variable composition — the proportions of components can be any value. A pure substance (element or compound) has fixed composition: water is always 88.81% oxygen and 11.19% hydrogen by mass, no matter where it comes from. This difference is why mass percent and mole fraction are needed to describe mixtures but not pure compounds.
facts.statement: Mixtures have variable composition; pure substances (elements and compounds) have fixed, definite composition.
facts.wowFactor: The law of definite proportions — pure compounds always have the same mass ratios — was discovered by Joseph Proust in 1799 and was one of the first quantitative laws of chemistry.
facts.visualDescription: Two containers — one with a pure blue substance in crystal form, one with a heterogeneous mixture of differently colored particles — labeled in pixel art

facts.quizQuestion: What quantity defines an element's identity and equals the number of protons in the nucleus?
facts.explanation: The atomic number (symbol Z) is the count of protons in an atom's nucleus. Every atom of a given element has the same atomic number — changing it would create a different element. For example, carbon always has Z = 6. Electrons can be gained or lost to form ions, but protons remain constant.
facts.sourceName: Khan Academy AP Chemistry — Atomic Structure

facts.quizQuestion: The mass number of an atom is the total count of which two types of subatomic particles in the nucleus?
facts.explanation: The mass number (A) = number of protons + number of neutrons. Electrons contribute negligible mass (~1/1836 of a proton). For carbon-14, A = 14 means 6 protons + 8 neutrons. The mass number is always a whole number, unlike atomic mass which is a weighted average across isotopes.

facts.quizQuestion: Isotopes of the same element are atoms that have the same number of protons but differ in what quantity?
facts.explanation: Isotopes share the same atomic number (same element identity) but have different numbers of neutrons, giving them different mass numbers. For example, carbon-12 has 6 neutrons and carbon-14 has 8 neutrons, but both have 6 protons. Isotopes of an element have nearly identical chemical properties because chemistry is governed by electrons, not neutrons.

facts.quizQuestion: Which electron configuration rule states that electrons fill orbitals starting with the lowest available energy level before moving to higher ones?
facts.explanation: The aufbau principle (from German 'aufbauen,' to build up) guides electron configuration writing. Electrons occupy the lowest-energy orbital available: 1s fills before 2s, which fills before 2p, and so on. The order follows the (n + l) rule: 4s fills before 3d because 4s has a lower energy in most neutral atoms.

facts.quizQuestion: Which quantum mechanical rule states that no two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers, meaning each orbital holds at most two electrons with opposite spins?
facts.explanation: The Pauli exclusion principle, formulated by Wolfgang Pauli in 1925, means every orbital can hold a maximum of two electrons, and those two must have opposite spins (spin-up and spin-down, or +½ and -½). This is why each s subshell holds 2 electrons, each p subshell 6, each d subshell 10, and each f subshell 14.

facts.quizQuestion: Which rule states that electrons occupy degenerate orbitals (equal-energy orbitals within the same subshell) singly before any pairing occurs, and all singly-occupied orbitals have the same spin?
facts.explanation: Hund's rule minimizes electron-electron repulsion by spreading electrons across degenerate orbitals. For nitrogen (1s²2s²2p³), all three 2p electrons occupy separate 2p orbitals with parallel spins before any orbital receives a second electron. This is why nitrogen is paramagnetic and why carbon's ground-state electron configuration has two unpaired electrons.

facts.quizQuestion: Nitrogen (atomic number 7) has the electron configuration 1s²2s²2p³. How many this value electrons does a neutral nitrogen atom have?
facts.explanation: Valence electrons are in the outermost principal energy level. For nitrogen, the valence shell is n = 2, containing 2 electrons in 2s and 3 electrons in 2p, giving 5 total valence electrons. This matches nitrogen's group number (Group 15 / Group VA) on the periodic table. These 5 valence electrons explain why nitrogen commonly forms 3 bonds (sharing 3) and can also act as a Lewis base.

facts.quizQuestion: Electrons in filled inner shells that do not participate in chemical bonding, shield valence electrons from nuclear charge, and appear in a noble gas shorthand bracket notation are called what?
facts.explanation: Core electrons are all electrons except those in the outermost (valence) shell. For sodium (1s²2s²2p⁶3s¹), the core is [Ne] = 1s²2s²2p⁶ (10 electrons); the single 3s electron is the valence electron. Core electrons shield valence electrons from the full nuclear charge, reducing effective nuclear charge (Zeff) experienced by valence electrons.

facts.quizQuestion: Chlorine has atomic number 17 and is in Period 3, Group 17. Which abbreviated (noble-gas core) electron configuration correctly represents a neutral chlorine atom?
facts.explanation: Chlorine (Z = 17) has 17 electrons. The noble gas core is [Ne] = 1s²2s²2p⁶ (10 electrons). The remaining 7 electrons fill 3s² (2) and then 3p⁵ (5). The abbreviated notation [Ne]3s²3p⁵ shows that chlorine has 7 valence electrons, matching Group 17, and needs just 1 more electron to achieve an octet — explaining its high reactivity.

facts.quizQuestion: A d subshell contains 5 orbitals. According to the Pauli exclusion principle, what is the maximum number of electrons a d subshell can hold?
facts.explanation: Each orbital holds at most 2 electrons (with opposite spins, per the Pauli exclusion principle). A d subshell has 5 orbitals (m_l = -2, -1, 0, +1, +2), so it can hold 5 × 2 = 10 electrons. This is why transition metals (which fill 3d, 4d, or 5d) can have up to 10 d electrons, and why elements like Zn ([Ar]4s²3d¹⁰) have a full d subshell.

facts.quizQuestion: In a photoelectron spectroscopy (PES) spectrum of an atom, each distinct peak corresponds to electrons ejected from a different what?
facts.explanation: PES fires high-energy photons (often X-rays or UV) at atoms. Electrons in different subshells (1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, etc.) require different amounts of energy to eject. Each peak in the resulting spectrum represents one subshell. The binding energy axis shows how tightly bound those electrons are, and the peak height reflects how many electrons occupy that subshell.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED, Unit 1 Topic 1.6

facts.quizQuestion: In a PES spectrum, electrons in subshells closer to the nucleus appear at what position relative to electrons in outer subshells?
facts.explanation: Electrons closer to the nucleus experience a stronger electrostatic attraction from the positive nuclear charge. More energy (higher binding energy) is required to remove them. In a PES spectrum, the horizontal axis is binding energy increasing to the left (or high to low). The 1s peak always appears at higher binding energy than 2s, which is higher than 2p, reflecting the energy cost to free inner electrons.
distractors: Higher kinetic energy of ejected electrons
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED, Unit 1 Topic 1.6

facts.quizQuestion: In a PES spectrum, the relative height (intensity) of each peak is proportional to what property of the corresponding this value?
facts.explanation: More electrons in a subshell means more ejected electrons at that binding energy, producing a taller peak. For example, sodium (1s²2s²2p⁶3s¹) would show: 1s peak height of 2, 2s peak height of 2, 2p peak height of 6 (the tallest), and 3s peak height of 1 (the shortest). Comparing peak heights lets chemists determine how many electrons occupy each subshell without knowing the element in advance.
distractors: Distance of the subshell from the nucleus
Principal quantum number of the subshell
Number of orbitals in the subshell
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED, Unit 1 Topic 1.6

facts.quizQuestion: Magnesium has the electron configuration 1s²2s²2p⁶3s². How many distinct this value would appear in the PES spectrum of a magnesium atom?
facts.explanation: Each distinct subshell in the electron configuration produces one PES peak. Magnesium (1s²2s²2p⁶3s²) has four occupied subshells: 1s, 2s, 2p, and 3s. Each subshell appears as a separate peak at its characteristic binding energy. The resulting spectrum has 4 peaks: the 2p peak is tallest (6 electrons), the 3s peak is shortest (2 electrons), and the 1s and 2s peaks are both height 2 but at higher binding energies.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED, Unit 1 Topic 1.6

facts.quizQuestion: A PES spectrum showing three peaks with relative heights of 2 : 2 : 5 from high to low binding energy can be used to identify which fundamental atomic property?
facts.explanation: The peak heights (2, 2, 5) correspond to the number of electrons in each subshell: 1s = 2, 2s = 2, 2p = 5. This gives total electrons = 9, identifying the element as fluorine (Z = 9) with configuration 1s²2s²2p⁵. PES is a direct experimental probe of electron configuration — the spectral pattern is a fingerprint of each element's unique electron arrangement.
facts.sourceName: College Board AP Chemistry CED, Unit 1 Topic 1.6

facts.quizQuestion: Moving left to right across a period on the periodic table, what happens to the first ionization energy of the elements?
facts.explanation: Across a period, the number of protons increases while electrons are added to the same shell, so shielding changes little but nuclear charge increases. This raises the effective nuclear charge (Zeff), pulling valence electrons closer and requiring more energy to remove them. Ionization energy generally increases left to right across a period, with slight dips at Group 2→3 and Group 5→6 due to subshell effects.

facts.quizQuestion: Moving down a group on the periodic table, what happens to the first ionization energy of the elements?
facts.explanation: Going down a group, valence electrons occupy higher principal energy levels (n = 1, 2, 3...) and are increasingly shielded from the nucleus by inner electron shells. The greater distance and increased shielding lower the effective nuclear charge experienced by valence electrons, making them easier to remove. This is why cesium (Cs) has a much lower ionization energy than lithium (Li), even though both are in Group 1.

facts.quizQuestion: Which element has the highest electronegativity on the Pauling scale, with a value of 3.98?
facts.explanation: Fluorine (F, atomic number 9) is the most electronegative element at 3.98 on the Pauling scale. Its small atomic radius and high nuclear charge create an intense pull on bonding electrons. This is why F-F bonds are relatively weak (the lone pairs on adjacent fluorines repel each other) and why HF is a weaker acid than HCl despite F being more electronegative.

facts.quizQuestion: Across a period from left to right (excluding noble gases), what is the general trend in electronegativity?
facts.explanation: Electronegativity measures an atom's ability to attract bonding electrons. Across a period, increasing nuclear charge (with little change in shielding) raises Zeff, so atoms pull bonding electrons more strongly. In Period 2: Li = 1.0, Be = 1.5, B = 2.0, C = 2.55, N = 3.0, O = 3.44, F = 3.98. Noble gases are typically excluded because they form few bonds.
distractors: Decreases for metals, increases for nonmetals
Increases then sharply decreases at Group 17

facts.quizQuestion: Moving left to right across a period on the periodic table, what happens to atomic?
facts.explanation: Across a period, electrons are added to the same valence shell while nuclear charge increases. The greater nuclear pull contracts the electron cloud, shrinking atomic radius. For example, in Period 2: Li (152 pm) > Be (112 pm) > B (87 pm) > C (77 pm) > N (75 pm) > O (73 pm) > F (64 pm). More protons pulling the same-shell electrons inward is the driving force.
distractors: Increases for metals, decreases for nonmetals

facts.quizQuestion: Moving down a group on the periodic table, what happens to atomic radius?
facts.explanation: Each period adds a new principal energy level. Valence electrons in higher n shells are, on average, farther from the nucleus and are more shielded by inner electrons. Despite increasing nuclear charge, the additional shielding and greater n value dominate, causing atomic radius to increase down a group. Li (152 pm) < Na (186 pm) < K (227 pm) < Rb (248 pm) < Cs (265 pm).
distractors: Decreases for metals, increases for nonmetals
Stays the same within a group

facts.quizQuestion: The net positive this value experienced by an electron after accounting for the shielding effect of inner electrons is called what?
facts.explanation: Effective nuclear charge (Zeff) = Z − shielding constant (S). Core electrons between a valence electron and the nucleus reduce the full nuclear charge felt by the valence electron. For sodium (Z = 11, [Ne]3s¹): the 10 core electrons roughly shield the 3s electron from 10 protons, so Zeff ≈ +1. Higher Zeff means stronger pull on valence electrons — this drives ionization energy, electronegativity, and atomic radius trends.

facts.quizQuestion: The reduction in the attractive force that a valence electron feels from the nucleus, caused by core electrons partially blocking the nuclear charge, is known as what?
facts.explanation: Core electrons (those in inner shells) repel outer valence electrons and partially cancel the attractive force of the nucleus. This is the shielding (or screening) effect. The more core electrons, the greater the shielding and the lower the Zeff experienced by valence electrons. s electrons shield more effectively than p electrons, which shield more than d, which shield more than f electrons.

facts.quizQuestion: Moving down a group on the periodic table, what happens to this value character?
facts.explanation: Metallic character measures how readily an atom loses electrons. Going down a group, increasing atomic radius and greater electron shielding lower Zeff on valence electrons, making them easier to remove. For example, in Group 14: carbon is a nonmetal, silicon and germanium are metalloids, and tin and lead are metals. The lowest ionization energies are in the lower-left of the periodic table (cesium, francium).
distractors: Peaks at the center of each group

facts.quizQuestion: Although fluorine is the most electronegative element, which of the two Period 2 and Period 3 halogens has a more negative (more favorable) first electron affinity, and why?
facts.explanation: Surprisingly, chlorine has a more negative electron affinity (−349 kJ/mol) than fluorine (−328 kJ/mol). Fluorine's very small 2p orbitals are crowded, so adding the 9th electron creates significant electron-electron repulsion that partially offsets the attractive nuclear force. Chlorine's larger 3p orbitals accommodate the extra electron with less repulsion. This is a classic exception to the simple electronegativity-drives-electron-affinity rule.

facts.quizQuestion: For main-group (representative) elements, what feature of the periodic table directly tells you the number of valence electrons in a neutral atom?
facts.explanation: For Groups 1–2 (s-block) and Groups 13–18 (p-block), the number of valence electrons equals the group number (using the 1–18 numbering: Group 1 = 1 valence electron, Group 14 = 4 valence electrons, Group 17 = 7 valence electrons, Group 18 = 8 valence electrons). This works because valence electrons are the electrons added beyond the previous noble gas core.
distractors: Principal quantum number of outermost subshell
Number of protons in the nucleus

facts.quizQuestion: When a neutral metal atom forms a cation (positively charged ion), what does it do to achieve a more stable electron configuration?
facts.explanation: Metal atoms have relatively low ionization energies and few valence electrons. They achieve noble-gas electron configurations by losing their valence electrons, forming cations. For example, sodium (Na, [Ne]3s¹) loses 1 electron to form Na⁺ ([Ne]), and magnesium (Mg, [Ne]3s²) loses 2 electrons to form Mg²⁺ ([Ne]). The resulting cation has fewer electrons than the original atom, giving it a net positive charge.

facts.quizQuestion: When a neutral nonmetal atom forms an anion (negatively charged ion), what does it do to achieve a noble-gas electron configuration?
facts.explanation: Nonmetal atoms have high electron affinities — gaining electrons releases energy because it fills their nearly-complete valence shell. Chlorine (Cl, [Ne]3s²3p⁵) gains 1 electron to form Cl⁻ ([Ar]), and oxygen (O, [He]2s²2p⁴) gains 2 electrons to form O²⁻ ([Ne]). The anion has more electrons than the original atom, giving it a net negative charge.

facts.quizQuestion: Calcium (atomic number 20, Group 2) forms the most stable ion by losing its 2 valence electrons. What is the charge on the calcium ion formed?
facts.explanation: Calcium's electron configuration is [Ar]4s². By losing both 4s electrons, Ca forms Ca²⁺ with the electron configuration of argon ([Ar]), a noble gas configuration. Group 2 alkaline earth metals characteristically form 2+ cations. Ca²⁺ is essential in human biology — it is the most abundant mineral in the body and is critical for bone structure, muscle contraction, and nerve signaling.

facts.quizQuestion: Sodium (Na⁺) and chloride (Cl⁻) ions combine in a 1:1 ratio to form an electrically neutral ionic compound. What is the chemical formula of this compound?
facts.explanation: Ionic compounds are electrically neutral overall. Na⁺ has a charge of +1 and Cl⁻ has a charge of −1. The charges cancel in a 1:1 ratio: one Na⁺ per one Cl⁻ gives a net charge of zero. The formula NaCl is written with the cation (Na) first. In the solid crystal, each Na⁺ is surrounded by 6 Cl⁻ neighbors and vice versa in a face-centered cubic lattice. NaCl melts at 801°C — evidence of strong ionic electrostatic attractions.

facts.quizQuestion: When the electronegativity difference between two bonding atoms exceeds 1.7, what type of chemical bond is formed?
facts.explanation: An electronegativity difference greater than 1.7 indicates that one atom attracts bonding electrons so strongly that electrons are effectively transferred rather than shared, producing an ionic bond. This threshold corresponds to roughly 50% ionic character in the bond.

facts.quizQuestion: A bond between two atoms with an electronegativity difference between 0 and 0.4 is classified as what type of bond?
facts.explanation: When electronegativity differences are very small (0–0.4), electrons are shared nearly equally and no significant dipole forms, producing a nonpolar covalent bond. The H–H bond (difference = 0) and C–H bond (difference ≈ 0.4) are classic examples.

facts.quizQuestion: What model describes metallic bonding as positively charged metal cations surrounded by a mobile 'sea' of delocalized valence electrons?
facts.explanation: In the electron sea model, metal atoms release their valence electrons into a shared pool. This delocalization explains metallic properties: the mobile electrons carry electrical current (conductivity), and the cations can slide past each other without breaking bonds (malleability and ductility).

facts.quizQuestion: When the electronegativity difference between two bonded atoms falls between 0.4 and 1.7, the bond is classified as what type?
facts.explanation: A polar covalent bond forms when electrons are shared unequally but not fully transferred. The more electronegative atom carries a partial negative charge (δ–) and the less electronegative atom carries a partial positive charge (δ+). The O–H bond (difference ≈ 1.4) in water is a familiar example.

facts.quizQuestion: Covalent bonds typically form between what combination of elements?
facts.explanation: Covalent bonds form when both atoms have similar electronegativity — typically when both are nonmetals. Because neither atom has a strong tendency to give up electrons entirely, they share them. Examples include O2, N2, H2O, and CO2, all composed of nonmetallic elements.

facts.quizQuestion: On a potential energy diagram for two bonding atoms, the distance at which potential energy reaches its minimum value is called what?
facts.explanation: As two atoms approach, potential energy decreases due to attractive forces until it reaches a minimum — the equilibrium bond length. At this distance, attractive and repulsive forces between the nuclei and electrons are balanced. Moving the atoms closer increases nuclear repulsion; moving them apart increases energy by stretching the bond.

facts.quizQuestion: Of all bond types between the same two atoms, which has the highest bond energy and the shortest bond length?
facts.explanation: Higher bond order means more electron density shared between nuclei, pulling them closer together (shorter bond length) and requiring more energy to break (higher bond energy). The N≡N triple bond (945 kJ/mol) is dramatically stronger than the N=N double bond (418 kJ/mol) or N–N single bond (163 kJ/mol).

facts.quizQuestion: Breaking a chemical bond is always what type of process with respect to energy?
facts.explanation: Bond breaking always requires an input of energy to overcome the electrostatic attraction holding the atoms together — it is always endothermic. Conversely, bond formation releases energy (exothermic). The net enthalpy change of a reaction reflects the balance between energy absorbed breaking reactant bonds and energy released forming product bonds.

facts.quizQuestion: As the bond order between two atoms increases from single to double to triple, what happens to the bond this value?
facts.explanation: Greater bond order means more shared electron pairs pulling the nuclei closer together, reducing internuclear distance. For carbon-carbon bonds: C–C ≈ 154 pm, C=C ≈ 134 pm, C≡C ≈ 120 pm. This inverse relationship between bond order and bond length is a core concept in AP Chemistry Unit 2.

facts.quizQuestion: Ionic compounds form a three-dimensional, repeating arrangement of oppositely charged ions called what?
facts.explanation: In an ionic solid, cations and anions alternate in a regular three-dimensional pattern called a crystal lattice. The geometry of the lattice (cubic, face-centered cubic, etc.) depends on the relative sizes and charges of the ions. This ordered structure maximizes electrostatic attractions and minimizes repulsions.

facts.quizQuestion: As the charges on ions in an ionic compound increase (e.g., going from NaCl to MgO), what happens to the lattice energy?
facts.explanation: Lattice energy is proportional to the product of ion charges. MgO has Mg²⁺ and O²– ions (charges ×2 and ×2), giving it a much higher lattice energy (3795 kJ/mol) than NaCl with its +1 and –1 charges (787 kJ/mol). Higher lattice energy means stronger ionic solid, higher melting point, and lower solubility in most solvents.

facts.quizQuestion: Ionic solids like NaCl shatter when struck because shifting layers brings like charges into alignment, causing repulsion. This makes them what?
facts.explanation: When a mechanical force shifts one layer of an ionic crystal relative to an adjacent layer, ions of the same charge line up and strongly repel each other, causing the crystal to cleave or shatter. This is in stark contrast to metals, where layers of atoms can slide past each other without disrupting the electron sea.

facts.correctAnswer: When dissolved in water or melted
acceptableAlternatives: In aqueous solution or molten state
distractors: Only when heated below melting point
Only when exposed to UV light
facts.quizQuestion: Ionic solids do not conduct electricity in the solid state, but they do conduct electricity under what conditions?
facts.explanation: In the solid state, ions are locked in the lattice and cannot migrate. When the compound is dissolved in water or melted, the ions are freed to move through solution or liquid, allowing electrical current to flow. This is why ionic compounds are called electrolytes — they conduct electricity when dissolved.

facts.quizQuestion: In brass, zinc atoms replace some copper atoms in the crystal lattice because the atoms are similar in size. What type of this value is brass?
facts.explanation: In a substitutional alloy, atoms of the added element are close in size to the host metal atoms (within ~15%) and replace them at lattice sites. Brass (copper-zinc) and bronze (copper-tin) are classic examples. The substituted atoms disrupt the regularity of the lattice, often increasing hardness compared to the pure metal.

facts.quizQuestion: Steel is formed when small carbon atoms occupy the gaps between iron atoms in the metal lattice. What type of this value is steel?
facts.explanation: In an interstitial alloy, small atoms (like carbon, nitrogen, or hydrogen) fit into the spaces between the larger host metal atoms rather than replacing them. Steel is the most important example: carbon atoms in the iron lattice impede dislocation movement, dramatically increasing hardness and tensile strength compared to pure iron.

facts.quizQuestion: Because the electron sea allows metal cation layers to slide past each other without breaking the bonding interaction, metals exhibit what physical properties?
facts.explanation: In metallic bonding, the mobile electron sea surrounds cations uniformly. When a force shifts one layer of cations relative to another, the delocalized electrons simply reflow around the new arrangement, maintaining the bond. This allows metals to be hammered into sheets (malleability) or drawn into wires (ductility) without fracturing.

facts.quizQuestion: The octet rule states that main-group atoms in Lewis structures tend to form bonds until they are surrounded by how many electrons?
facts.explanation: The octet rule reflects the stability of having a full valence shell — analogous to the electron configuration of the nearest noble gas. Most second-period nonmetals (C, N, O, F) strictly obey the octet rule. Hydrogen is a notable exception, needing only two electrons (a duet) to complete its 1s shell.

facts.quizQuestion: Which molecule is a classic example of an electron-deficient octet exception, where the central atom has only six electrons around it in the Lewis structure?
facts.explanation: Boron has only three valence electrons and forms three bonds with fluorine in BF3, giving boron just six electrons — an incomplete octet. Boron's small size and inability to accommodate more than four bonds makes it electron-deficient. This makes BF3 a potent Lewis acid, readily accepting an electron pair from Lewis bases.

facts.quizQuestion: Which molecule has an expanded octet with 12 electrons around the central sulfur atom, requiring d-orbital participation to accommodate six bonding pairs?
facts.explanation: Sulfur in SF6 forms six bonds with fluorine atoms, placing 12 electrons around sulfur. This is possible because sulfur is in the third period and has accessible d orbitals that can expand its valence shell beyond eight electrons. SF6 is an extremely stable, dense, and electrically insulating gas used in high-voltage equipment.

acceptableAlternatives: Add electrons equal to the negative charge
Add extra electrons for negative charge
distractors: Remove one electron per negative charge
Ignore the charge when counting electrons
Add one bond per negative charge
Remove one bond per negative charge
Add a lone pair to the central atom only
Distribute charge equally across all atoms
Subtract one electron for each bond formed
facts.quizQuestion: When drawing the Lewis structure of a negatively charged polyatomic ion like NO3–, how do you account for the negative this value in the total electron count?
facts.explanation: Each unit of negative charge on a polyatomic ion represents one extra electron beyond what the neutral atoms contribute. For NO3– (charge = –1), you add 1 electron to the sum of valence electrons from N (5) and the three O atoms (3 × 6 = 18), giving 5 + 18 + 1 = 24 total electrons to distribute in the Lewis structure.

facts.quizQuestion: In a Lewis structure, pairs of valence electrons that belong to a single atom and are not shared between atoms are called what?
facts.explanation: Lone pairs (also called non-bonding pairs) are electron pairs that remain on one atom and do not participate in covalent bonding. They are critical for determining molecular geometry in VSEPR theory because they occupy space and repel bonding pairs, compressing bond angles. Water has two lone pairs on oxygen.

facts.quizQuestion: Which triatomic molecule with a bent shape has two equivalent resonance structures because the double bond can be drawn on either oxygen–oxygen bond?
facts.explanation: Ozone (O3) cannot be represented accurately by a single Lewis structure because both O–O bonds are experimentally identical (bond length ≈ 128 pm), intermediate between a single bond (148 pm) and double bond (121 pm). Two resonance structures are drawn and the actual molecule is a resonance hybrid with delocalized electron density equally distributed over both bonds.

facts.correctAnswer: valence e⁻ − lone pair e⁻ − ½ bonding electrons
acceptableAlternatives: Valence electrons minus lone pair electrons minus half the bonding electrons
distractors: Valence electrons minus total bonding electrons
Total electrons minus lone pair electrons
Bonding electrons minus lone pair electrons
Valence electrons minus all surrounding electrons
facts.quizQuestion: The formal charge on an atom in a Lewis structure is calculated as what?
facts.explanation: Formal charge = (valence electrons of free atom) – (lone pair electrons on atom) – ½(bonding electrons shared with atom). It assigns bonding electrons equally between partners. The preferred Lewis structure minimizes formal charges and places negative formal charges on more electronegative atoms.

facts.quizQuestion: When a molecule is best described by multiple Lewis structures of equal or similar energy, the actual bonding involves what — electrons that are spread over more than two atoms?
facts.explanation: Resonance structures indicate that electrons in pi bonds are not confined between any one pair of atoms but are delocalized over the entire resonance system. In benzene, for example, the six pi electrons are delocalized over all six carbons, making all C–C bonds equivalent at 140 pm (intermediate between single and double). Delocalization lowers the molecule's energy below any single Lewis structure.

distractors: Maximizes the number of lone pairs
Places all electrons on the central atom
Gives the central atom an octet first
Places positive charge on the most electronegative atom
Maximizes bond order on terminal atoms
facts.quizQuestion: When choosing among multiple valid Lewis structures for the same molecule, the preferred (most accurate) structure is the one that does what with this value charges?
facts.explanation: The preferred Lewis structure minimizes formal charges, ideally giving every atom a formal charge of zero. When nonzero formal charges are unavoidable, negative formal charges should reside on more electronegative atoms. For CO2, the structure with C=O double bonds (all atoms at formal charge 0) is preferred over the structure with a C–O single bond and C≡O triple bond (which creates nonzero formal charges).

facts.quizQuestion: CH4 has four bonding pairs and no lone pairs around carbon. According to VSEPR theory, what is the molecular geometry of methane?
facts.explanation: Four electron domains arranged to minimize repulsion point toward the corners of a tetrahedron. With no lone pairs, the molecular geometry matches the electron geometry exactly — tetrahedral, with H–C–H bond angles of 109.5°. This geometry also describes NH4+ and CCl4.

facts.quizQuestion: NH3 has three bonding pairs and one lone pair on nitrogen. What is the molecular geometry of ammonia?
facts.explanation: Although the four electron domains in NH3 adopt a tetrahedral arrangement, the molecular geometry describes only atom positions — three H atoms form the base of a pyramid with N at the apex. The lone pair compresses the H–N–H bond angles to 107°, slightly less than the tetrahedral ideal of 109.5°, because lone pairs exert stronger repulsion than bonding pairs.

facts.quizQuestion: A carbon atom in ethylene (C2H4) has three electron domains — two C–H bonds and one C=C bond. What this process does this carbon adopt?
facts.explanation: Three electron domains require three hybrid orbitals, formed by mixing one s and two p orbitals — called sp2 hybridization. The three sp2 orbitals point toward the corners of a triangle at 120° (trigonal planar geometry). The unhybridized p orbital perpendicular to this plane overlaps with the adjacent carbon's p orbital to form the pi bond of the double bond.

facts.correctAnswer: One sigma bond and one pi bond
acceptableAlternatives: A sigma bond and a pi bond
distractors: One sigma bond and two pi bonds
Two sigma bonds and one pi bond
facts.quizQuestion: A double bond between two atoms consists of how many this value bonds and how many pi bonds?
facts.explanation: Every covalent bond, regardless of order, contains exactly one sigma (σ) bond formed by direct, end-on orbital overlap along the bond axis. Double bonds add one pi (π) bond formed by side-on (lateral) overlap of parallel p orbitals. Triple bonds consist of one σ and two π bonds. Pi bonds are weaker than sigma bonds because lateral overlap is less effective than axial overlap.

facts.quizQuestion: SF6 has six bonding pairs and no lone pairs around the central sulfur atom. What molecular geometry does SF6 adopt?
facts.explanation: Six electron domains minimize repulsion by pointing toward the faces of an octahedron — four in a square plane plus one above and one below. With no lone pairs, molecular geometry matches electron geometry: octahedral, with all F–S–F bond angles of 90°. SF6 requires sp3d2 hybridization of the sulfur atom.

facts.quizQuestion: What type of intermolecular force exists between ALL molecules, arising from temporary fluctuations in electron density?
facts.explanation: London dispersion forces (LDF) arise from instantaneous dipoles created by momentary uneven electron distribution. They are present in every molecule — polar or nonpolar — and are the ONLY IMF between nonpolar molecules like N2, O2, and noble gases. LDF strength increases with molar mass and molecular surface area.
facts.statement: London dispersion forces are present in all molecules and arise from temporary, instantaneous dipoles caused by fluctuating electron distributions.
facts.wowFactor: Noble gases like argon can only become liquid because of London dispersion forces — the weakest IMF that makes condensed matter possible at all.
facts.visualDescription: Two nonpolar molecules glowing with shifting electron clouds creating flickering temporary dipole arrows, dark dungeon lab setting

facts.quizQuestion: London dispersion forces increase in strength as which property of a molecule increases?
facts.explanation: Larger molar mass means more electrons, which means larger, more polarizable electron clouds. More polarizable clouds create stronger instantaneous dipoles, leading to stronger LDF. This explains why I2 (solid at room temperature) has much higher boiling point than F2 (gas) despite both being nonpolar diatomic molecules.
facts.statement: London dispersion force strength increases with increasing molar mass because larger molecules have more electrons and more polarizable electron clouds.
facts.wowFactor: The boiling point trend for halogens (F2 < Cl2 < Br2 < I2) is entirely explained by increasing LDF — not any difference in polarity.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison of small and large electron cloud glowing spheres with stronger attraction arrows for the larger one, pixel art alchemist lab

facts.quizQuestion: What type of intermolecular force occurs between polar molecules due to the attraction of their permanent positive and negative ends?
facts.explanation: Dipole-dipole forces occur between polar molecules that have a permanent dipole moment. The partially positive end (δ+) of one molecule aligns with the partially negative end (δ−) of a neighboring molecule. These forces are stronger than LDF for similar-sized molecules but weaker than hydrogen bonding.
facts.statement: Dipole-dipole forces are attractive interactions between the permanent dipoles of polar molecules — the δ+ end of one molecule attracts the δ− end of another.
facts.wowFactor: HCl boils at −85°C while the heavier but nonpolar Ar boils at −186°C — that 100-degree difference is almost entirely due to dipole-dipole forces.
facts.visualDescription: Two polar molecules with δ+ and δ− labels aligned end-to-end with glowing attraction lines, pixel art chemistry dungeon

facts.correctAnswer: hydrogen bonded to F, O, or N
acceptableAlternatives: H bonded to fluorine, oxygen, or nitrogen
H attached to F, O, or N
H directly bonded to a highly electronegative atom
distractors: hydrogen bonded to any electronegative atom
hydrogen bonded to chlorine or sulfur
hydrogen bonded to phosphorus or sulfur
molecules with O-H and C=O groups
facts.quizQuestion: What structural requirement must a molecule meet to participate in this value bonding as the donor?
facts.explanation: Hydrogen bonding requires a hydrogen atom covalently bonded directly to fluorine (F), oxygen (O), or nitrogen (N). These three elements are so electronegative that the H becomes very electron-poor (δ+), allowing it to interact strongly with lone pairs on adjacent F, O, or N atoms. H bonded to Cl or S does NOT qualify — they are not electronegative enough.
facts.statement: Hydrogen bonding requires a hydrogen atom covalently bonded directly to F, O, or N — the three most electronegative elements that create a sufficiently electron-poor H for strong dipole interactions.
facts.wowFactor: Water's anomalously high boiling point (100°C vs. the expected ~−80°C for a molecule its size) is almost entirely explained by hydrogen bonding between H and O.
facts.visualDescription: Water molecules with glowing dashed hydrogen bond lines connecting O-H...O, surrounded by glowing F, O, N labels on a dark alchemist backdrop

facts.quizQuestion: What is the correct order of intermolecular force strength from weakest to strongest: London dispersion forces, hydrogen bonding, and the force between partial charges?
facts.explanation: London dispersion forces are the weakest IMF because they depend on temporary, fleeting dipoles. Dipole-dipole forces are stronger because they involve permanent dipoles with consistent alignment. Hydrogen bonding is the strongest of these three because it involves the extreme electronegativity of F, O, or N creating an especially large dipole and an unusually close, directional interaction.
facts.statement: The relative strength of IMFs from weakest to strongest is: London dispersion forces < dipole-dipole forces < hydrogen bonding.
facts.wowFactor: Hydrogen bonds are 10–40 times stronger than typical London dispersion forces — they're strong enough to give DNA its double-helix structure and water its life-enabling properties.
facts.visualDescription: Three force arrows of increasing size labeled LDF, dipole-dipole, and hydrogen bond pointing right in a glowing RPG skill tree style on dark background

facts.quizQuestion: Stronger intermolecular forces require more energy to overcome, which directly increases a substance's what?
facts.explanation: Boiling point is determined by how much energy is required to overcome IMFs and convert a liquid to a gas. Stronger IMFs (hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > LDF) mean higher boiling points. For example: H2O (hydrogen bonding) boils at 100°C, HCl (dipole-dipole) at −85°C, and CH4 (only LDF) at −161°C.
facts.statement: Stronger intermolecular forces lead to higher boiling points because more energy is required to overcome those forces and convert the substance from liquid to gas.
facts.wowFactor: Water's boiling point of 100°C is about 180°C higher than you'd predict based on molecular size alone — all thanks to hydrogen bonding.
facts.visualDescription: A thermometer rising beside molecules with strong IMF arrows, bubbles forming as it crosses the boiling threshold, pixel art dungeon lab

facts.quizQuestion: What type of intermolecular force explains why NaCl dissolves readily in water — the attraction between Na+ ions and the δ− oxygen of water molecules?
facts.explanation: Ion-dipole forces arise between an ion (cation or anion) and a polar molecule. Na+ is attracted to the δ− end (oxygen) of water molecules, while Cl− is attracted to the δ+ end (hydrogen). These forces are typically the strongest type of IMF and are responsible for the dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
facts.statement: Ion-dipole forces are the attractive interactions between an ion and a polar molecule, and are responsible for dissolving ionic compounds in polar solvents like water.
facts.wowFactor: Ion-dipole forces are strong enough to pull apart an ionic crystal lattice — overcoming the powerful lattice energy of NaCl requires the combined energy of billions of ion-dipole interactions.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing Na+ ion surrounded by oriented water molecules with δ− oxygen pointing toward the ion, pixel art dungeon alchemy scene

facts.quizQuestion: Weaker intermolecular forces allow more molecules to escape from the liquid surface, resulting in a higher what at a given temperature?
facts.explanation: Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by a vapor in equilibrium with its liquid. Weaker IMFs mean molecules need less energy to escape the liquid surface, so more molecules are in the gas phase at any given temperature — giving a higher vapor pressure. Diethyl ether (weak LDF) has a much higher vapor pressure than water (strong H-bonding) at the same temperature.
facts.statement: Substances with weaker intermolecular forces have higher vapor pressures because molecules can more easily escape from the liquid phase into the gas phase.
facts.wowFactor: The explosive volatility of diethyl ether (used as early anesthesia) compared to water comes down entirely to IMFs — ether has only LDF while water has hydrogen bonding.
facts.visualDescription: Molecules escaping a liquid surface into vapor phase with pressure gauge rising, weak IMF molecules escaping more readily, pixel art lab dungeon

facts.quizQuestion: What type of solid is characterized by a high melting point, brittleness, and the ability to conduct electricity only when dissolved in water or melted?
facts.explanation: Ionic solids consist of cations and anions held together in a crystal lattice by strong electrostatic forces. This gives them high melting points (must overcome lattice energy), brittleness (shifting layers brings like charges together, causing repulsion and fracture), and conductivity only in solution or molten form (ions must be mobile to carry charge).
facts.statement: Ionic solids have high melting points due to strong lattice forces, are brittle, and conduct electricity only when dissolved or melted — when ions become mobile.
facts.wowFactor: NaCl's lattice energy (~787 kJ/mol) is so large that it requires nearly as much energy to melt table salt as to boil a large pot of water.
facts.visualDescription: A crystalline salt lattice with alternating glowing positive and negative ions, a crack showing its brittle nature, pixel art dungeon mine backdrop

facts.quizQuestion: What type of solid is held together by intermolecular forces rather than chemical bonds, resulting in low melting points and softness? (Examples: ice, dry ice, iodine crystals)
facts.explanation: Molecular solids consist of discrete molecules held together by IMFs (LDF, dipole-dipole, or hydrogen bonding). Because IMFs are much weaker than ionic, covalent, or metallic bonds, molecular solids have low melting points and are soft. Ice is a molecular solid held by H-bonds; dry ice (CO2) by LDF and dipole-dipole forces.
facts.statement: Molecular solids are held together by intermolecular forces (not chemical bonds), giving them low melting points, softness, and poor electrical conductivity.
facts.wowFactor: Dry ice (solid CO2) sublimes at just −78.5°C because only weak London dispersion and dipole-dipole forces hold the CO2 molecules together in the solid.
facts.visualDescription: Ice crystals with faint dashed H-bond lines between molecules, soft and low-energy appearance, pixel art dungeon ice cave

facts.quizQuestion: Diamond, quartz (SiO2), and silicon carbide (SiC) are examples of what type of solid, characterized by extremely high melting points and a continuous lattice of this value bonds?
facts.explanation: Covalent network solids consist of atoms connected throughout the entire material by covalent bonds — there are no discrete molecules. To melt these solids, you must break covalent bonds, requiring enormous amounts of energy. Diamond has a melting point around 3550°C. SiO2 (quartz) melts at ~1650°C. SiC is used industrially as an abrasive.
facts.statement: Covalent network solids (diamond, SiO2, SiC) consist of atoms connected by covalent bonds throughout the entire lattice, giving them extremely high melting points and hardness.
facts.wowFactor: Diamond is the hardest naturally occurring material because every carbon atom is covalently bonded to four others in a rigid 3D network — there's no weak point anywhere in the structure.
facts.visualDescription: Diamond crystal lattice with glowing covalent bonds in every direction, pixel art gemstone dungeon with extremely high temperature indicator

facts.quizQuestion: What type of solid is malleable, ductile, and conducts electricity and heat in the solid state due to a 'sea of delocalized electrons'?
facts.explanation: Metallic solids consist of metal cations surrounded by delocalized electrons (the 'electron sea' model). The mobile electrons allow conductivity of electricity and heat. The non-directional metallic bonds allow layers to slide past each other without breaking (malleable/ductile), unlike brittle ionic solids where layer shifts bring like charges together.
facts.statement: Metallic solids are malleable, ductile, and conduct electricity in the solid state because their delocalized 'sea of electrons' allows both layer movement and charge transfer.
facts.wowFactor: Copper can be drawn into wire thinner than a human hair because its metallic bonds have no fixed directional character — the electron sea accommodates any reshaping.
facts.visualDescription: Metal cations in a grid surrounded by a glowing sea of electrons flowing around them, pixel art forge dungeon with copper-colored tones

facts.quizQuestion: A solid sample conducts electricity and can be hammered into a thin sheet without shattering. What type of solid is it?
facts.explanation: This describes a metallic solid. Conductivity in the solid state rules out ionic solids (which only conduct when molten or dissolved) and molecular/network solids (which don't conduct). Malleability rules out ionic (brittle) and covalent network (extremely hard and brittle). Only metallic solids have both properties due to their delocalized electrons and non-directional bonding.
facts.statement: A solid that conducts electricity and is malleable must be a metallic solid — only delocalized electrons enable both properties simultaneously.
facts.wowFactor: Identifying solid type from properties alone is an AP exam skill — the combination of conductivity (solid state) + malleability is diagnostic for metallic solids.
facts.visualDescription: A shiny metal ingot being hammered flat with electricity sparks flowing through it, pixel art dungeon smithy

acceptableAlternatives: when vapor pressure equals external pressure
distractors: vapor pressure exceeds the critical pressure
all molecules have the same kinetic energy
facts.quizQuestion: A liquid boils when what thermodynamic condition is met at its surface?
facts.explanation: Boiling occurs when a liquid's vapor pressure equals the external (atmospheric) pressure. At this point, bubbles of vapor can form throughout the liquid, not just at the surface. This is why water boils at 100°C at sea level (1 atm) but at only ~90°C at high altitude where atmospheric pressure is lower.
facts.statement: A liquid boils when its vapor pressure equals the atmospheric (external) pressure — at this point vapor bubbles can form throughout the liquid.
facts.wowFactor: At the top of Mt. Everest (pressure ~0.33 atm), water boils at only 70°C — pasta cooks slower and you'd need a pressure cooker to make a proper cup of tea.
facts.visualDescription: A beaker of boiling liquid with vapor pressure arrows equal to atmospheric pressure arrows, bubbles forming throughout the liquid, pixel art lab

acceptableAlternatives: temperature is constant during phase change
facts.quizQuestion: On a heating curve, what happens to temperature while a substance is undergoing a phase change (melting or boiling)?
facts.explanation: During a phase change, all energy input goes into breaking intermolecular forces (potential energy increases) rather than increasing molecular motion (kinetic energy stays constant). Since temperature is a measure of average kinetic energy, temperature stays constant throughout the phase change. This creates the flat plateaus on a heating curve.
facts.statement: During a phase change on a heating curve, temperature remains constant because all energy goes into breaking IMFs (increasing potential energy), not increasing kinetic energy.
facts.wowFactor: The flat plateau on a heating curve is why adding ice to a drink keeps it at exactly 0°C until all the ice melts — the melting process absorbs heat without any temperature rise.
facts.visualDescription: A heating curve graph with flat plateau at melting and boiling points, labeled with phase change zones, glowing dungeon chalkboard style

facts.quizQuestion: Dry ice (solid CO2) transforms directly from solid to gas without passing through the liquid phase. What is this phase transition called?
facts.explanation: Sublimation is the direct phase transition from solid to gas, bypassing the liquid state. It occurs when the atmospheric pressure is below a substance's triple point pressure. For CO2, the triple point is at 5.1 atm — since atmospheric pressure is only 1 atm, CO2 goes directly from solid to gas at standard conditions. The reverse process (gas → solid) is called deposition.
facts.statement: Sublimation is the direct phase transition from solid to gas, occurring when atmospheric pressure is below the substance's triple point pressure (as with dry ice at 1 atm).
facts.wowFactor: Dry ice sublimes at −78.5°C at 1 atm, making it perfect for fog effects and keeping things frozen — the CO2 gas produced is also heavier than air and sinks, creating that eerie low-lying fog.
facts.visualDescription: A block of dry ice with white CO2 vapor rising directly from the solid, skipping liquid phase, pixel art dungeon stage with fog effects

facts.quizQuestion: As the temperature of a liquid increases, what happens to its vapor pressure?
facts.explanation: As temperature increases, more molecules have enough kinetic energy to overcome intermolecular forces and escape into the gas phase. This increases the number of molecules in the vapor phase, raising the vapor pressure. The relationship is exponential (described by the Clausius-Clapeyron equation), which is why vapor pressure rises steeply with temperature.
facts.statement: Vapor pressure increases as temperature increases because more molecules have sufficient kinetic energy to escape the liquid surface into the gas phase.
facts.wowFactor: The Clausius-Clapeyron equation reveals that vapor pressure doesn't just increase with temperature — it grows exponentially, which is why a small temperature rise dramatically accelerates evaporation.
facts.visualDescription: A graph of vapor pressure vs temperature with an upward exponential curve, molecules escaping faster at higher temperatures, dungeon lab backdrop

facts.quizQuestion: What phase transition involves a gas converting directly to a solid without passing through the liquid state? (The process that forms frost on a cold window)
facts.explanation: Deposition is the reverse of sublimation — water vapor in the air converts directly to ice crystals (frost) on a cold surface without first becoming liquid water. This occurs when the surface temperature is below both the dew point and 0°C. Snowflakes also form by deposition of water vapor onto dust particles in clouds.
facts.statement: Deposition is the phase transition from gas directly to solid (the reverse of sublimation), exemplified by the formation of frost from water vapor on cold surfaces.
facts.wowFactor: Each snowflake forms by deposition — water vapor molecules directly crystallizing onto a tiny dust particle — which is why snowflakes have such intricate hexagonal symmetry based on water's crystal structure.
facts.visualDescription: Water vapor arrows pointing downward onto a freezing cold glass surface forming frost crystals, pixel art winter dungeon scene

facts.quizQuestion: What equation relates the pressure, volume, moles, and temperature of an ideal gas?
facts.explanation: The ideal gas law PV = nRT combines Boyle's, Charles's, and Avogadro's laws into one equation. P = pressure (atm), V = volume (L), n = moles, R = gas constant (0.08206 L·atm/mol·K), T = temperature (always in Kelvin). It assumes gas particles have no volume and no interactions — a good approximation at low pressure and high temperature.
facts.statement: The ideal gas law PV = nRT relates pressure (P), volume (V), moles (n), gas constant (R), and temperature (T in Kelvin) for an ideal gas.
facts.wowFactor: The ideal gas law was assembled in 1834 by Émile Clapeyron from the earlier gas laws of Boyle, Charles, Gay-Lussac, and Avogadro — unifying centuries of experimental observations into a single equation.
facts.visualDescription: The equation PV = nRT glowing in gold on a dungeon stone wall surrounded by gas molecule diagrams and a pressure gauge

facts.quizQuestion: What is the value of the ideal gas constant R when pressure is measured in atmospheres and volume in liters?
facts.explanation: The gas constant R = 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K when using liters and atmospheres. The same constant expressed in SI units is R = 8.314 J/mol·K (or Pa·m³/mol·K). When solving PV = nRT problems, always use R = 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K if pressure is in atm, and ensure temperature is in Kelvin.
facts.statement: The ideal gas constant R = 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K (or equivalently 8.314 J/mol·K). Temperature in PV = nRT must ALWAYS be in Kelvin.
facts.wowFactor: The value of R was first determined experimentally by combining measurements from multiple gas law experiments — it represents a universal proportionality constant that applies to all ideal gases.
facts.visualDescription: The letter R glowing with the value 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K inscribed below it, surrounded by gas law variables on a dungeon chalkboard

facts.quizQuestion: What is the molar volume of an ideal gas at STP (0°C and 1 atm)?
facts.explanation: At STP (standard temperature and pressure: 0°C = 273.15 K and 1 atm), one mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 L (derived directly from PV = nRT: V = nRT/P = 1 × 0.08206 × 273.15 / 1 = 22.414 L). This value applies to ANY ideal gas regardless of identity — 1 mol He, 1 mol CO2, and 1 mol Ar all occupy 22.4 L at STP.
facts.statement: At STP (0°C and 1 atm), one mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 L — the molar volume at STP, derived directly from PV = nRT.
facts.wowFactor: The fact that 22.4 L/mol applies equally to tiny He atoms and massive CO2 molecules (which are 11× heavier) is a direct demonstration of Avogadro's principle — equal volumes of gases at the same T and P contain equal numbers of molecules.
facts.visualDescription: A 22.4 L container glowing with one mole label, various gas molecules inside all equal in number, dungeon alchemy lab setting

facts.quizQuestion: Which gas law states that pressure and volume are inversely proportional at constant temperature, expressed as P₁V₁ = P₂V₂?
facts.explanation: Boyle's Law (1662) states that at constant temperature and amount, the pressure and volume of a gas are inversely proportional: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. This makes intuitive sense — compressing a gas into a smaller volume forces more collisions per unit area (higher pressure). Boyle's Law is a special case of the ideal gas law with n and T constant.
facts.statement: Boyle's Law states that for a fixed amount of gas at constant temperature, P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ — pressure and volume are inversely proportional.
facts.wowFactor: Robert Boyle discovered this law in 1662 using a J-shaped tube with trapped air — one of the first quantitative experiments in chemistry, establishing the pattern of testing gas behavior mathematically.
facts.visualDescription: A piston compressing gas in a cylinder with P and V arrows showing inverse relationship, glowing P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ equation, dungeon lab

facts.quizQuestion: Which gas law states that volume and temperature are directly proportional at constant pressure, expressed as V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂?
facts.explanation: Charles's Law states that at constant pressure and amount, the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its temperature (in Kelvin): V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂. As temperature increases, gas molecules move faster and exert more force, requiring the container to expand to maintain constant pressure. Temperature MUST be in Kelvin — using Celsius gives wrong results.
facts.statement: Charles's Law states that at constant pressure, V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂ — volume and absolute temperature (in Kelvin) are directly proportional.
facts.wowFactor: Charles's Law explains why a sealed bag of chips expands when you take it to high altitude and why hot air balloons work — heated air expands, becomes less dense, and provides lift.
facts.visualDescription: A balloon expanding as temperature increases, with V/T ratio arrows labeled, dungeon forge with heating coils glowing red

facts.quizQuestion: What temperature and pressure define Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP) in AP Chemistry?
facts.explanation: STP for AP Chemistry is defined as 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm. Note: IUPAC updated their STP to 0°C and 100 kPa (1 bar) in 1982, but College Board AP Chemistry continues to use 0°C and 1 atm. At this AP definition of STP, the molar volume of an ideal gas is 22.4 L/mol.
facts.statement: STP as used in AP Chemistry is 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm, giving an ideal gas molar volume of 22.4 L/mol.
facts.wowFactor: There are actually two 'STPs' — the AP Chemistry version (0°C, 1 atm) and the modern IUPAC version (0°C, 1 bar = 100 kPa). They're close but not identical, giving slightly different molar volumes (22.4 vs 22.7 L/mol).
facts.visualDescription: A thermometer at 0°C and a pressure gauge at 1 atm side by side, labeled STP, in a dungeon alchemy lab pixel art scene

facts.quizQuestion: According to Kinetic Molecular Theory, temperature is directly proportional to which property of gas particles?
facts.explanation: KMT states that the average kinetic energy of gas particles is directly proportional to absolute temperature: KE_avg = (3/2)kT, where k is Boltzmann's constant (1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K) and T is temperature in Kelvin. This means that at the same temperature, all gases — regardless of molar mass — have the same average kinetic energy. Heavier gases move more slowly to compensate.
